q_activity.label: Triggering activity
q_activity.prompt: Which activity triggers your knee pain the most?

q_location_squat.label: Pain location during squats
q_location_squat.prompt: Where does your knee hurt most when squatting?

q_location_stairs.label: Pain location on stairs
q_location_stairs.prompt: Where does your knee hurt most when going down stairs?

q_location_running.label: Pain location when running
q_location_running.prompt: Where does your knee hurt most when running?

dx_squat_anterior.explanation: Front-of-knee pain during squats usually means the patellofemoral joint is taking more load than it can tolerate right now.

recommendations.title: Limit squat depth with a box or chair
recommendations.description: Squat to a box that keeps pain at or below 3/10. This reduces compression on the front of the knee.

recommendations.title: Elevate heels during squats
recommendations.description: Place a small wedge or plate under your heels to shift load away from the kneecap.

recommendations.title: Why squats aggravate the front of the knee
recommendations.description: Deep knee flexion increases patellofemoral compression. Reducing depth temporarily lets the area calm down.

dx_squat_medial.explanation: Inner knee pain during squats often comes from the medial compartment being loaded unevenly, especially if the knees drift inward.

recommendations.title: Use a band around the knees
recommendations.description: Place a light resistance band just above the knees and push outward during squats to reduce medial collapse.

recommendations.title: Widen your squat stance slightly
recommendations.description: A slightly wider stance with toes turned out can reduce stress on the inner knee.

dx_squat_lateral.explanation: Outer knee pain during squats can indicate IT band tension or lateral compartment loading.

recommendations.title: Narrow your squat stance slightly
recommendations.description: Bring your feet slightly closer together to reduce lateral compartment load.

recommendations.title: Side-lying hip abduction
recommendations.description: Lie on your side and slowly lift the top leg 15-20 times. Strengthening the hip abductors reduces lateral knee stress.

dx_stairs_anterior.explanation: Going downstairs loads the front of the knee heavily because your quad has to control the descent. The patellofemoral joint absorbs most of that force.

recommendations.title: Go downstairs sideways
recommendations.description: Turn sideways and step down leading with the less painful leg. This reduces the quad demand on the sore knee.

recommendations.title: Use the railing to offload
recommendations.description: Press lightly on the handrail as you descend to take 10-20% of the load off your knee.

recommendations.title: Why stairs hurt the front of the knee
recommendations.description: Descending stairs produces up to 3.5x bodyweight through the patellofemoral joint. Temporary modifications let it settle.

dx_stairs_medial.explanation: Inner knee pain on stairs can come from the medial meniscus or collateral ligament being loaded during the twisting motion of each step.

recommendations.title: Keep your knee over your second toe
recommendations.description: Focus on tracking your knee directly over your foot rather than letting it collapse inward on each step.

recommendations.title: Single-leg balance holds
recommendations.description: Stand on the affected leg for 30 seconds, 3 times daily. This improves the stability that protects the inner knee.

dx_stairs_lateral.explanation: Outer knee pain on stairs may involve the IT band or lateral structures being compressed during the descent phase.

recommendations.title: Slow your stair descent
recommendations.description: Take stairs one at a time at a slower pace. Rushing increases lateral knee forces.

recommendations.title: Lateral band walks
recommendations.description: Place a band around your ankles and take 10 steps sideways each direction. This strengthens the muscles that stabilize the outer knee.

dx_running_anterior.explanation: Front-of-knee pain during running is commonly runner's knee (patellofemoral syndrome). It often responds well to cadence and load adjustments.

recommendations.title: Increase your step rate by 5-10%
recommendations.description: Shorter, quicker steps reduce the braking force on each stride and lower patellofemoral load.

recommendations.title: Reduce downhill running temporarily
recommendations.description: Downhill running increases kneecap compression. Stick to flat routes until pain settles.

recommendations.title: Understanding runner's knee
recommendations.description: Patellofemoral pain in runners usually resolves with load management. You often don't need to stop running — just adjust.

dx_running_medial.explanation: Inner knee pain during running can stem from foot mechanics, hip weakness, or medial compartment overload.

recommendations.title: Shorten your stride slightly
recommendations.description: A slightly shorter stride reduces the rotational forces through the inner knee.

recommendations.title: Clamshell exercise
recommendations.description: Lie on your side with knees bent. Open the top knee like a clamshell, 15 reps x 3 sets. This strengthens the hip rotators that protect the inner knee.

dx_running_lateral.explanation: Outer knee pain when running often involves the iliotibial band. It typically flares at a specific distance and responds well to load modification.

recommendations.title: Run below your flare-up distance
recommendations.description: If pain starts at 3 km, run 2 km for a week, then add 10% weekly. Stay below the pain threshold.

recommendations.title: Avoid cambered road surfaces
recommendations.description: Running on a sloped road tilts your pelvis and increases IT band tension. Choose flat paths or alternate sides.

recommendations.title: Standing hip abduction
recommendations.description: Stand on one leg and slowly lift the other leg out to the side, 12 reps x 3 sets. Hip strength reduces IT band load.
